justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Domain-architecture conflict explicitly identified in escalations section — PostgreSQL cannot structurally enforce staleness. (2) Formal escalation with specific questions about fleet size, update frequency, Redis operational expertise, and Bird's tolerance for edge cases. (3) Three architectural options presented (PG-only, Redis+PG, PG with materialized view) with clear trade-offs. (4) trade_offs section includes sacrifices for each decision (e.g., Redis adds operational complexity, async writes create brief inconsistency window). (5) Risks include PostgreSQL write throughput ceiling explicitly. (6) MJ does not commit to a final design without missing information — escalation is formal and recommendation is conditional. (7) Confidence level is 55, well below 70 threshold.

observations.text: Escalation is thorough and well-structured, with three concrete options and a clear recommendation that defers to domain authority (Bird) for final call
Confidence level of 55 appropriately reflects the significant unknowns (fleet size, ping frequency, team Redis expertise)
Trade-offs are genuinely balanced — each decision lists sacrifices, not just gains
PostgreSQL write throughput risk explicitly called out with concrete mitigation (batching, partitioning, TimescaleDB upgrade path)
The TTL-based staleness enforcement is a creative architectural insight that makes the domain invariant structurally unbreakable
Output is very long and detailed — while thorough, MJ could have been more concise while still hitting all criteria
Low confidence areas explicitly list the missing information needed before committing to a design
